hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Sprinter W906 van — from the 2.1-litre four-cylinder diesel to the 3.5-litre V6 petrol. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the OM651 that powered the UK's favourite workhorse. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [PATTERN DATA].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes-Benz Sprinter W906 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W906 Sprinter specifically — across the full 2006–2018 production run, covering diesel, petrol and LPG variants.

engines.variants: 310 CDI, 313 CDI, 316 CDI, 416 CDI, 513 CDI, 516 CDI

overview.intro: The Mercedes-Benz Sprinter W906, also known as the NCV3 (New Concept Van 3), is the second-generation Sprinter, introduced in Europe in 2006 as the successor to the T1N (W901–W905). Production ran until 2018, when it was superseded by the W907. The W906 was offered in a vast range of body styles — panel vans, chassis cabs, minibuses and crew cabs — with two weight classes (2500 and 3500) and multiple wheelbase and roof height options. The engine range was one of the most diverse of any Sprinter generation, spanning the OM646 2.1-litre four-cylinder diesel (2006–2009), the OM651 2.1-litre four-cylinder diesel (2009–2018), the OM642 3.0-litre V6 diesel (2006–2018), the M272 3.5-litre V6 petrol (2006–2010), and the M271 1.8-litre turbo petrol (2008–2013), the latter also available as a bi-fuel LPG variant (316 NGT).
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz W906 (NCV3) • Predecessor: T1N (W901–W905) • Successor: W907 • Sibling body styles in this generation: Van, Chassis Cab, Minibus, Crew Cab.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W906 Sprinter attracts strong specialist interest in the UK commercial vehicle market, with OM651 diesel and OM642 V6 diesel enquiries dominating fleet maintenance and high-mileage repair planning [PATTERN DATA].

items.quote: Thin-Data Fallback mode applied — compressed to a single paragraph. Best & Worst Engines: The W906's engine range splits into three distinct tiers. At the top sits the OM651 2.1-litre four-cylinder diesel — a modern, efficient and proven workhorse that powered the majority of UK Sprinters from 2009 onwards, offering strong fuel economy and excellent longevity. The OM646 2.1-litre diesel (2006–2009) is similarly durable, though older and less refined. The OM642 3.0-litre V6 diesel is a legend — smooth, powerful and effortless for heavy loads — but the oil cooler seals are a well-documented weakness requiring significant labour to rectify. The M272 3.5-litre V6 petrol is a rare choice in the UK — smooth and powerful but thirsty, and balance shaft wear on pre-2008 examples is a known issue. The M271 1.8-litre turbo petrol (including the LPG-fuelled NGT variant) offers lower running costs for urban use but is less common in the UK. For most buyers, the OM651 diesel represents the sweet spot — the UK's favourite Sprinter engine, with proven durability and manageable running costs. Who it's for: commercial operators and fleet buyers who need the sweet spot between economy, reliability and whole-life cost.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Injector wear, EGR valve, DPF clogging
rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid Workhorse — proven, parts widely available

rows.commonMajorFailure: Oil cooler seal leaks, swirl flap motor, turbo wear
rows.ownershipVerdict: V6 Choice — smooth and powerful, oil cooler seals a known cost
Rare Petrol — smooth but thirsty, specialist-only

rows.commonMajorFailure: Timing chain stretch, turbo wear, LPG system (NGT)
rows.ownershipVerdict: NGT Option — lower fuel costs, complex LPG system

economicsRule.title: The W906 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,000 OM651 recon against a £4,000–£12,000 W906 sits at 25–125% of the van's value. On a sub-£5,000 high-mileage example, that exceeds our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold — walk away. Against a well-maintained £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to 38–62% — a repair decision, not a financial one. For OM642 V6 vans, the economics are similar — a £4,500 recon on a £10,000 example is 45% — viable. Value the van first, but remember that a well-maintained Sprinter holds its value better than most commercial vehicles.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W906 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM651 diesel — excellent fuel economy, proven durability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W906 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM642 V6 diesel — more complex, higher parts costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W906 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM651 (313 CDI/316 CDI) — the sweet spot between performance and running costs.

problems.title: OM642 V6 Oil Cooler Seal Leaks
problems.affectedModels: All OM642-engined W906 Sprinters — 318 CDI, 418 CDI, 319 CDI, 419 CDI, 519 CDI.
problems.rootCause: The OM642 V6's oil cooler seals (located in the "V" of the engine) degrade over time, leading to oil leaks that can drip onto the exhaust and cause smoke. Labour-intensive to replace due to engine access — the intake manifold and other components must be removed.
problems.recommendation: Have the oil cooler seals inspected at every major service — an £800–£1,500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,500+ replacement that follows oil starvation.

cta.label: Compare W906 OM642 oil cooler seal repair quotes →

problems.title: OM651/OM646 Injector and EGR Valve Wear
problems.affectedModels: OM646 and OM651-engined W906 Sprinters — 209–516 CDI variants.
problems.rootCause: Injector wear and EGR valve clogging are common on high-mileage Sprinters, particularly those used for stop-start urban deliveries. Injector failure causes rough running and loss of power; EGR valve failure triggers the engine management light and can lead to DPF issues.
problems.recommendation: Have injectors and the EGR valve inspected at every major service — a £400–£900 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,800+ replacement that follows a failure.

cta.label: Compare W906 injector and EGR repair quotes →

problems.title: M271 Timing Chain Stretch (Petrol/LPG Variants)
problems.rootCause: The M271's timing chain tensioner can fail, allowing the chain to stretch — cold-start rattle is the classic early warning. If ignored, the chain can skip, causing catastrophic engine damage.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle on a petrol or LPG W906, get the timing chain inspected immediately — an £800–£1,500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500+ replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare W906 M271 timing chain repair quotes →

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Wear (Pre-2008 Petrol V6)
problems.rootCause: The M272 V6's balance shaft gears can wear prematurely, causing the timing to shift and triggering the engine management light. This is a well-documented issue on early M272 engines.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a 2006–2008 petrol W906, check for the balance shaft issue — a £1,000–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,000+ replacement that follows a failure.

cta.label: Compare W906 M272 balance shaft repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: W906 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz Sprinter W906 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned W906 engine typically costs between £2,000 and £6,500 supply only [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet], depending on engine — the OM646/OM651 four-cylinder diesels sit at £2,000–£5,000, and the OM642 V6 diesel at £3,200–£6,500. Fitting adds 8–16 hours of labour at specialist rates — budget an additional £520–£1,040 for installation. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz Sprinter W906 engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The OM651 2.1-litre four-cylinder diesel (313 CDI/316 CDI) is widely regarded as the most durable W906 engine — a modern, efficient and proven workhorse that powered the majority of UK Sprinters from 2009 onwards, offering strong fuel economy and excellent longevity. The OM646 2.1-litre diesel (2006–2009) is similarly durable, though older and less refined. The OM642 V6 diesel is also robust but the oil cooler seals are a well-documented maintenance cost.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz Sprinter W906?
items.answer: Oil cooler seal leaks on the OM642 V6 diesel (318 CDI/319 CDI), injector and EGR valve wear on the OM646/OM651 four-cylinder diesels, timing chain stretch on the M271 petrol/LPG engines (316 NGT), and balance shaft wear on the M272 V6 petrol (524, pre-2008) are the most documented issues. Each requires different preventative strategies.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz Sprinter W906 reliable?
items.answer: Yes — the W906 is widely regarded as one of the most reliable Sprinter generations. The OM651 diesel is a proven workhorse that regularly exceeds 200,000 miles with proper maintenance. The OM646 is similarly durable. The OM642 V6 diesel is robust but the oil cooler seals require attention at higher mileages. Overall, a well-maintained W906 is a dependable commercial vehicle.

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz Sprinter W906 engine should I avoid?
items.answer: No engine outright — but the OM642 V6 diesel requires careful oil cooler seal history checking. The M272 V6 petrol (524) is rare and thirsty, with balance shaft issues on pre-2008 examples. The M271 1.8-litre turbo (316 NGT) can suffer from timing chain stretch and the LPG system adds complexity. For most UK buyers, the OM651 four-cylinder diesel is the sensible choice.

items.question: How do I know if my W906 engine needs replacing?
items.answer: Oil leaks from the V6 (OM642), rough running or loss of power (injectors/EGR on OM646/OM651), cold-start rattle (M271 timing chain), balance shaft-related engine management lights (M272), or blue smoke from the exhaust are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a W906 engine, or should I scrap the van?
items.answer: Depends on the van's value. Against a well-kept £8,000–£12,000 Sprinter, a £3,500–£5,000 OM651 recon is 29–62% of the van's value — safe. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon is 87–125% — walk away. For OM642 V6 vans, a £4,500–£6,500 recon on a £10,000 example is 45–65% — viable. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line. A well-maintained Sprinter holds its value better than most commercial vehicles.

items.question: How long do Mercedes-Benz Sprinter W906 engines last?
items.answer: The OM651 diesel can comfortably exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing — many examples have passed 300,000 miles. The OM646 is similarly durable. The OM642 V6 diesel is robust but oil cooler seals typically require replacement at 80,000–150,000 miles. The M271 and M272 petrol engines are less common in the UK and typically last 120,000+ miles.

items.question: What is the difference between the W906 and W907?
items.answer: The W906 is the second-generation Sprinter (also known as NCV3, 2006–2018, this page), while the W907 is its successor (2018–present). The W907 introduced a completely new platform, more modern engines (OM654 four-cylinder diesel replacing the OM651), a new gearbox and significantly upgraded safety features. Parts availability for the W906 remains strong due to its popularity.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz Sprinter W906 engine code?
items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on OM646/OM651/OM642/M272/M271 units. Tell us your variant and approximate year and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.
